24.1.label: Question 24.1
24.1.scenario: A consulting actuary specialises in providing advice on health and care issues. They have been asked to advise a national charity providing help and support to sufferers of arthritis and their families. The trustees of the charity have become increasingly concerned about the difficulties their members have in deciding upon the best course of treatment from among those offered by their medical advisors. The trustees have heard of the term 'quality of active life years' and asked how this type of statistical indicator could help them in providing information to help their members in reaching decisions about their treatment.
24.1.question: N/A

(i).question: Describe the points that would be made in the reply, which should mention any problems that might arise in using these indicators. (You are not expected to comment on sources of data.)

How statistical indicators can be used: Arthritis is a chronic and often progressive condition, but is not usually life threatening.
Patients will be more interested in their quality of life, rather than just their chances of dying.
Individuals seeking treatment will be concerned about the relative effect on their subsequent lifestyle of the different treatments on offer (including the effect of having no treatment).
The best treatment for the patient would be that which gives the greatest expected quality of life in each year of future lifetime.
The expected quality outcome for can be assessed on a scale of 0 (dead) to 1 (perfect health) for each future ('quality-adjusted') year.
The outcome measures for each treatment possibility can be displayed on a simple graph.

The outcomes can be summarised by taking the sum of the future 'quality-adjusted year', allowing for expected mortality following the treatment. This gives an expectation of life in 'quality-adjusted life years' (QALYs) for each treatment.
Patients who are paying for their own treatment may be concerned to choose a cost-effective treatment.
Information on the cost per QALY for each treatment may help in the decision making process.
Problems: Although the QALY figure has the benefit of simplicity, this may be too straightforward for use in making such important decisions.
Different patients will have different views on what quality of life they would accept and for how long.
Judgement is required when deciding on the quality measures (ie the scale 0 to 1).
Various methods exist of trying to derive these - none of which are entirely objective.
The data presented gives the average outcome for the procedure. We would need to recognise that there would be a range around this.
For example, the outcomes may differ substantially depending on, for example, the age at the date of treatment or the severity of the symptoms at the time of treatment.
This range could be narrowed to an extent by calculating the statistics based on the unique characteristics of the patient involved.
This would allow for their expected outcome, given relevant factors such as age, gender, medical history and lifestyle factors.
This would be subject to there being sufficient data at the required level of detail.
The cost of the calculation may also be prohibitive.
However, for treatments where these differences are substantial the trustees could consider presenting separate information for each sub-group of patients.

If required, an individual calculation might also allow for the patient's own perception of quality of life, and so how they would relate a certain degree of disability on the scale of 0 to 1, but this might be difficult to do in practice.
The statistics may conflict with what the patients' medical advisors are recommending - for example, they may be using a different measure of what is 'best' - and this may cause difficulties.
The trustees need be aware of these issues when deciding on the statistics to give members and what advice to give with them.
They should emphasise that the information is for guidance and is not a prescription for a decision on treatment, and they should avoid any impression that they are making recommendations.
To this end the trustees might be well advised to provide a 'help-line' to answer questions from and offer counselling to their members.

(ii).question: Describe some potential applications of summary measures, such as 'quality of active life years', in the context of healthcare systems generally.

Applications of summary measures: Summary measures of population health are measures that combine information on mortality and non-fatal health outcomes to represent population health as a single number.
Possible uses for such measures include:

Comparing the health of one population to that of another population, which: allows evaluations of the performance of different health systems; enables decision-makers to focus their attention on those health systems with the worst performance; provides the possibility of analysing the key contributors to differences in health between populations.
Comparing the health of the same population at different points in time.
Monitoring over time is essential in the evaluation of a healthcare system's performance and progress towards its stated objectives.

Identifying and quantifying overall health inequalities within populations, for example, they might be used to identify whether particular sectors of a population are receiving a better quality healthcare compared to others.
Providing appropriate and balanced attention to the effects of non-fatal health outcomes on overall population health, rather than the focus being on conditions that primarily cause mortality.
Informing debates on priorities for health service delivery and planning, eg to help a health agency or government develop a short-list of national healthcare priorities.

Informing debates on priorities for research and development investment in the health sector, eg by considering the relative contributions of different diseases, injuries and risk factors to the total summary measure.
Improving professional training curricula in public health.
Analysing the benefits of health interventions for use in cost-effectiveness analyses, eg using cost per QALY measures to decide the best course of treatment as discussed above.

24.2.label: Question 24.2
24.2.scenario: A team of consultant actuaries have been asked to advise the Ministry of Health, which is part of the government of a developing country, on the establishment of its national healthcare system. The government has been recently elected and there are elections every two years. The country has no previous history of State provision of healthcare, and is expecting a period of rapid economic progress in the near future. The consultants have found the reports produced by the World Health Organisation useful in preparing their advice.
24.2.question: N/A

(i).question: Discuss the possible objectives of the government in providing an appropriate healthcare system.

Objectives of the government: According to WHO, the three fundamental objectives of healthcare systems are: improving the health of the population they serve; responding to people's expectations; providing financial protection against the costs of ill-health.

In meeting these objectives, the government should try not only to achieve the best attainable level (ie 'goodness'), but also to have the smallest feasible differences between individuals and groups (ie 'fairness').

'Fairness' involves ensuring that all people are treated in the same way (eg not excluding certain sectors from benefits). It may have an objective of redistributing wealth so that the wealthy subsidise the poor.

The government should bear in mind the need to act in line with the style of politics and the culture of people within the country. It may have promised healthcare reform at the last election, and so need to keep this political promise.
If these objectives are met, and are seen to be met during the next two years, this will increase its chances of meeting the overall aim of retaining power at the next election.

The health programme should increase the number of people actively in work so that the government may expect higher future tax revenues, which could ultimately help recover the costs of the programme.
In addition, a healthy population should, in general, be more productive, and so help boost the country's economy.
There may be additional economic benefits of the proposal (such as generating jobs or attracting investment from overseas) that the government is hoping to achieve.
However, the extent to which the government can achieve these aims will depend on the resources available, and so this may be difficult for a developing country.
It will need to budget carefully, ensuring it has sufficient income to meet the benefits promised. It will aim to maximise efficiencies in the system in order to keep expenses as low as is practical.

(ii).question: Describe the matters that the Ministry of Health should consider in order to achieve these aims.

Matters to consider: The Ministry needs to consider what its priorities should be in achieving the government's objectives.
It may set specific goals to focus on, for example: to improve infant mortality/morbidity, or to combat specific diseases, eg AIDS or malaria.

Its priorities will depend on factors such as: the significance of achievement of the goal to the nation's overall level of health; practical issues, such as what can be improved quickly; political influences, such as what the population wants (or has been promised).
In particular, if elections occur every two years, the government may seek targets that provide significant and popular short-term benefits quickly (eg improvements in maternity services).

The Ministry should consider other factors that can influence the level of health within a population, which may also need to be addressed to meet the government's aims - for example, access to water, sanitation, food and shelter.

In particular, it should be aware of the two-way causal relationship between economic development and health. Addressing low poverty may improve general health, which in turn, will improve economic growth.

Economic growth may be 'expected', but this may depend on the extent to which the intended improvements in health actually take place. On the other hand, if the expected economic growth is not achieved, this may restrict the establishment of the intended healthcare system.

The effectiveness of the proposed system will depend on other factors, which must be allowed for in establishing the system. These include: the level of education in the population; cultural factors, such as the role of women in the society; religious influences, such as refusal to have certain treatments; the infrastructure in the country, eg the transport, communication and electricity networks.
In order to successfully meet its aims (according to the WHO), the government must take the ultimate responsibility for the performance of the country's healthcare system.

It is recommended that the Ministry takes on a large part of the stewardship of the system, ie the supervision, monitoring and enforcement of the government's policies. This stewardship should involve all sectors of society (eg private hospitals, insurers, infrastructure and education providers).
At a high level, then, a decision needs to be taken on what types of healthcare will be provided as part of the proposed system.

This is likely to include provision of medical treatment, eg from hospitals. However, consideration should be given to other means of healthcare provision, such as: primary care ie advice and treatment from GPs; welfare benefits, eg payments when off-work due to sickness or disability; long-term care provision.
It should also consider the role of less-direct measures, such as education and preventative medicine (eg screening programmes), as a way of improving health.

For benefits involving monetary amounts (eg welfare benefits), it needs to decide the form of the benefit payments, eg whether the benefit should be: a lump sum or a regular income; flat rated or earnings related.
It may also need to decide how benefit levels will increase over time, eg in line with an inflation index. Note that inflation may be high or unpredictable in a developing country.
For each type of healthcare provided, the Ministry needs to consider any eligibility criteria and the ease of access to provision (or benefits).

The Ministry may need to prioritise certain services and it needs to decide, in detail, the criteria for rationing services. It should ensure that it is not excluding whole groups of the population from treatment (eg discriminating).
It should consider how, and to what extent, the government will work with the private healthcare sector, eg it may use private hospitals to provide some State-funded treatments.

Also, it should consider the role of private insurance in the provision of healthcare, and to what extent this meets the government's objectives - for example, if insurance is not available to all, this may be seen as being 'unfair' or elitist.
On the other hand, private insurance may be a way to increase funding for healthcare, to increase system capacity and to encourage its citizens to take responsibility for their own healthcare.
However, for a developing country, private health insurance is likely to account for only a small proportion of total health expenditure (at least initially).

The cost of setting up and running the system required needs to be estimated, and compared with the available resources. Decisions on the best way to finance the system need to be taken (see part (iii) below).

The Ministry needs to consider ways of minimising expenses of the system - for example, through setting 'budgets' for specific healthcare providers within the system and making them accountable for keeping to them (eg NHS trusts in the UK).
Consider practical issues, such as the buildings, equipment and staff required. It will need to ensure that the staff are adequately trained.

It should consider the communication and relationships between different parts of the healthcare system. For example, it should decide on the role of GPs as 'gatekeepers' in referring patients for further treatment.
The considerations in the above paragraphs are particularly important because, historically, they have caused failings in establishment of various healthcare systems.
The Ministry needs to consider and quantify the risks involved in establishing the system, eg it should consider what to do if the cost of the system turns out to be more expensive than expected.
Consider whether any expected macro-economic benefits of the proposal (eg reducing unemployment levels) will be achieved, and how will these be measured.

Consider how the new proposals are to be communicated to the public. This should be done in a way that: makes the public aware of the system and makes it appears attractive; manages the expectations of the public, which the system is trying to meet.

It needs to consider the administration required - for example, the procedures necessary to check eligibility to healthcare provision or benefits. The system needs to be seen as being fair, and ways of measuring this should also be considered.

The Ministry should be aware of the factors influencing the demand for healthcare, which may affect the public's expectations of the system, such as changes in: demographics, eg an ageing population; technological developments, eg in treatments and communication; social trends, such as education and income levels.

Consider how the system compares with those of other countries, particularly those in a similar stage of development (or other members of the same trading organisation). The Ministry may wish to establish reciprocal arrangements with certain other countries.

(iii).question: Outline the points that would be made in the report covering: approaches to financing the system; measurement of population health levels, and how these measurements might be used.

Approaches to financing the system: To ensure that all individuals have access to health services, three interrelated functions of health system financing are crucial: revenue collection; pooling of resources; purchasing of interventions (ie medical services).
These three functions are often integrated in a single organisation, although there may be a separation between revenue and purchasing.
Revenue collection may be via: general taxation; mandated social health insurance contributions; voluntary private health insurance contributions; out-of-pocket payments and donations.
The Ministry will need to decide to what extent the first two sources should be used to finance a State-funded scheme.
Social health insurance contributions are likely to be salary-related and almost never risk-related.

The Ministry needs to decide on precise details, such as: thresholds and limits on contribution levels; the level (if any) of contribution to be made by employers; how to deal with gaps in contribution histories, eg should people who are full-time carers be entitled to healthcare?; whether individuals will be required to make a contribution towards certain services (eg coinsurance); whether certain citizens will be excluded from certain services, eg means testing.

However, out-of-pocket payments and donations may be an important complement to state-funded sources of revenue for healthcare in this country. By receiving donations (eg from charities), the government may be under obligation to take the donors' needs into consideration.
The government may consider giving tax incentives to encourage private provision, and so reduce the burden on the state system.

The government will need to decide on (and find) sufficient capital to make the initial investment required (eg to build hospitals). It may consider working with private companies, eg using a public-private partnership initiative.

Pooling is the accumulation and management of revenues in such a way as to ensure that the risk of having to pay for healthcare is borne by all the members of the pool and not by each contributor individually.

Pooling reduces uncertainty for both citizens and providers. By increasing and stabilising demand and the flow of funds, pooling can increase the likelihood that patients will be able to afford services, and that a higher volume of services will occur and thereby lead to new provider investments.
The scheme may be forward funded or pay-as-you go.
Purchasing is the process by which pooled funds are paid to healthcare providers in order to deliver a specified or unspecified set of health interventions (services).

This can be performed: passively, which implies following a predetermined budget or simply paying bills when presented; strategically, which means actively choosing interventions in order to achieve the best performance, by means of selective contracting and incentive schemes.
Measurement of population health levels: Population health levels should be measured allowing for mortality and non-fatal health outcomes.
This level can be represented as a single number by a summary measure. An example is the Quality Adjusted Life Years (QALY).

Such measures could include consideration of: infant mortality rates; life expectancies; cause of death profiles (eg percentage of deaths in the population attributable to specified diseases and causes); prevalence of disability (eg percentage of population with moderate to severe disability).
Such a measure will be more complicated to calculate than one based on life expectancies only. Also, its value is dependent on the weight assigned to each type of disability.
Related socio-economic conditions could also be considered in the measure - eg the proportions of households with water, sanitation or a direct energy supply.
Such measures can be used for:

comparing the health of one population to that of another population, which allows evaluations of the performance of different health systems and enables the key contributors to differences in health between populations to be analysed
monitoring the health of the same population over time, so that healthcare system's performance and progress towards its stated objectives can be evaluated
identifying and quantifying overall health inequalities within populations
providing appropriate and balanced attention to the effects of non-fatal health outcomes on overall population health, rather than the focus being on conditions that primarily cause mortality
informing debates on priorities for health service delivery and planning, eg to help the Ministry develop a short-list of national healthcare priorities

informing debates on priorities for research and development investment in the health sector, eg by considering the relative contributions of different diseases, injuries and risk factors to the total summary measure
improving professional training curricula in public health
analysing the benefits of health interventions for use in cost-effectiveness analyses, eg using cost per QALY measures to decide the best course of treatment
